mnemonics.title: WEAK Entity Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: W-E-A-K: W-ithout (no primary key), E-xists (only with owner), A-ttributes (partial key + descriptive), K-ind (of entity) - depends on owner K-ind

mnemonics.title: Double Notation Reminder
mnemonics.content: Weak entities are ALWAYS double: Double Rectangle for entity, Double Diamond for relationship, Double Line for total participation - Think of it as 'doubly' dependent!

mnemonics.title: Partial Key Location
mnemonics.content: PARTIAL KEY = DASHED underline; FULL KEY = SOLID underline - DASHED = Discriminator (partial), SOLID = Strong (complete)

acronyms.usage: ER modeling is the foundation for understanding weak entities
Weak entities don't have their own PK, they use owner PK + partial key
Typical cardinality ratio in identifying relationships from owner to weak entity

visualTips.visualization: Picture a weak entity as a 'nested box' - rectangle inside another rectangle. The outer box represents dependency, inner box represents the entity itself. Think of it as 'entity within entity'. The dashed underline for partial key can be remembered as 'temporary' - because it's not a complete key on its own.

Visualize the double diamond as a 'strong bond' - two diamonds fused together. The double line on weak entity side shows 'total commitment' - the entity cannot exist without the relationship. It's like being 'chained' to the owner.

Think of partial key like a seat number on a specific flight. The flight number (owner key) alone identifies the flight, but you need the seat number (partial key) to identify the specific passenger. The combination (flight + seat) uniquely identifies the passenger.

analogies.analogy: Think of a house (strong entity) vs a room in a house (weak entity). A room cannot exist without a house - it has no independent address. The house address + room number uniquely identifies each room. If you demolish the house (delete owner), the rooms cease to exist.

Consider a book in a library. Multiple copies of the same book exist. The ISBN (owner's key) identifies the book title, but you need the copy number (partial key) to identify the specific physical book you can borrow. The ISBN + copy number uniquely identifies each copy.

Think of a parent-child relationship. A child (weak entity) is identified by their name (partial key) combined with their parent's surname (owner's key). The child cannot exist independently in terms of family identity - they are identified through their parent.

Imagine a subscription to a magazine. The subscription (weak entity) must always have a subscriber (owner). You cannot have a subscription floating without a subscriber - the relationship is total. If you cancel the subscription, the subscriber record remains, but the subscription is deleted.
